attributes.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with an 11-month history of amenorrhoea, accompanied by hot flushes, night sweats, and difficulty sleeping. She reports that these symptoms are significantly affecting her quality of life. She has a medical history of hypertension, which is well-controlled on three medications, and cholelithiasis. She underwent a lumpectomy for stage I oestrogen receptor-positive breast cancer eight years ago, followed by radiotherapy and five years of tamoxifen. She completed her tamoxifen course three years ago and has been cancer-free since then. She does not take any regular medications apart from her antihypertensives. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. She asks about the possibility of starting hormone replacement therapy (HRT) to alleviate her menopausal symptoms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate recommendation regarding hormone replacement therapy for this patient?
options: Initiate combined HRT with close monitoring
Avoid HRT due to breast cancer history
Begin HRT with transdermal oestrogen only
attributes.explanation:
This case presents a complex clinical scenario involving a post-menopausal woman with multiple factors to consider when deciding on hormone replacement therapy (HRT). The most critical factor in this patient's history is her previous oestrogen receptor-positive breast cancer. Despite being in remission for several years, this history is an absolute contraindication to HRT. The risk of cancer recurrence outweighs the potential benefits of symptom relief.

Avoiding HRT due to breast cancer history is the most appropriate recommendation. Even though the patient completed her cancer treatment and has been cancer-free for three years, the risk of recurrence with HRT is significant, especially given that her cancer was oestrogen receptor-positive. Current guidelines advise against HRT in women with a history of breast cancer, regardless of how long they have been in remission.

Initiating combined HRT with close monitoring is not appropriate due to the patient's history of breast cancer. No amount of monitoring can mitigate the increased risk of cancer recurrence associated with HRT in this population.

Starting oestrogen-only HRT is not suitable for two reasons: firstly, it's contraindicated due to the patient's breast cancer history, and secondly, oestrogen-only HRT is typically only recommended for women who have had a hysterectomy, which this patient has not.

Beginning HRT with transdermal oestrogen only might be considered in some patients with cardiovascular risk factors, as it has a lower risk of thromboembolism compared to oral formulations. However, in this case, it's still contraindicated due to the history of breast cancer.

Commencing HRT with tibolone, a synthetic steroid with oestrogenic, progestogenic, and androgenic effects, is also contraindicated in women with a history of breast cancer. While it might be used in some post-menopausal women, it's not suitable for those with a history of hormone-sensitive cancers.

It's worth noting that while the patient's hypertension is well-controlled, it would still be a factor to consider carefully if HRT were otherwise appropriate. The history of cholelithiasis is not a contraindication to HRT but might influence the choice of administration route if HRT were being considered.

For this patient, non-hormonal options for managing menopausal symptoms should be explored. These might include lifestyle modifications, cognitive behavioral therapy for sleep issues, and possibly medications like selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) or gabapentin for vasomotor symptoms. A referral to a menopause specialist might be appropriate to discuss all available options.
learningPoints: Hormone replacement therapy is contraindicated in patients with a history of hormone-sensitive breast cancer, even if they are in remission.
The decision to use HRT in patients with well-controlled cardiovascular risk factors requires careful consideration of individual risks and benefits.
A history of hysterectomy influences the type of HRT that can be used, but is not a contraindication.
references: Australian Menopause Society. What is menopausal hormone therapy and is it safe? https://www.menopause.org.au/health-info/fact-sheets/what-is-menopausal-hormone-therapy-mht-and-is-it-safe Accessed 19/09/2020.

RANZCOG C-GYN 16. Menopausal Hormone Therapy Advice. Reviewed 2015. https://ranzcog.edu.au/RANZCOG_SITE/media/RANZCOG-MEDIA/Women%27s%20Health/Statement%20and%20guidelines/Clinical%20-%20Gynaecology/Menopausal-Hormone-Therapy-Advice-(C-Gyn-16)-Re-write-July-2015.pdf?ext=.pdf Accessed 19/10/2020.
Wentzensen N, Trabert B. Hormone therapy: short-term relief, long-term consequences. The Lancet. 2015;385(9980):1806-8.

Gramling R, Eaton CB, Rothman KJ, Cabral H, Silliman RA, Lash TL. Hormone replacement therapy, family history, and breast cancer risk among postmenopausal women. Epidemiology. 2009 Sep;20(5):752-6. doi: 10.1097/EDE.0b013e3181a71279. PMID: 19451819; PMCID: PMC2903620.

Australian Government Cancer Australia. Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT) and risk of breast cancer. 2010. Available from: https://www.canceraustralia.gov.au/resources/position-statements/hormone-replacement-therapy-hrt-and-risk-breast-cancer
